**Sunday Silence** (March 25, 1986 – August 19, 2002) was an [American](/wiki/United_States "United States")-bred [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") racehorse and [sire](/wiki/Horse_breeding#Terminology "Horse breeding"). In 1989, he won the [Kentucky Derby](/wiki/Kentucky_Derby "Kentucky Derby") and the [Preakness Stakes](/wiki/Preakness_Stakes "Preakness Stakes") but failed to complete the Triple Crown when he was defeated in the [Belmont Stakes](/wiki/Belmont_Stakes "Belmont Stakes"). Nevertheless, he won the [Breeders' Cup Classic](/wiki/Breeders%27_Cup_Classic "Breeders' Cup Classic") and was voted [American Champion Three-Year-Old Colt](/wiki/Eclipse_Award_for_Outstanding_3-Year-Old_Male_Horse "Eclipse Award for Outstanding 3-Year-Old Male Horse") and [American Horse of the Year](/wiki/Eclipse_Award_for_Horse_of_the_Year "Eclipse Award for Horse of the Year") that same year. Sunday Silence's racing career was marked by his rivalry with [Easy Goer](/wiki/Easy_Goer "Easy Goer"), whom he had a three to one edge over in their head-to-head races.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Easy Goer, the 1988 [American Champion Two-Year-Old Colt](/wiki/American_Champion_Two-Year-Old_Colt "American Champion Two-Year-Old Colt") finished second to Sunday Silence in the Kentucky Derby, the Preakness, and the Breeders' Cup Classic. However, Easy Goer prevailed by eight [lengths](/wiki/Horse_length "Horse length") in the Belmont, denying Sunday Silence the Triple Crown. Both horses were later voted into the [American Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Racing_and_Hall_of_Fame "National Museum of Racing and Hall of Fame").

After his retirement from racing, Sunday Silence attracted little support by breeders in the United States and was exported to Japan. He was the leading [sire](/wiki/Horse_breeding#Terminology "Horse breeding") in Japan on thirteen occasions, surpassing the previous record of ten titles by [Northern Taste](/wiki/Northern_Taste "Northern Taste"). Although the relatively insular nature of Japanese racing at the time meant that Sunday Silence's success was initially restricted to his home territory, his descendants have in recent years won major races in Australia, France, the United Kingdom, Hong Kong, the United States and Dubai.[[3]](#cite_note-3) [*Blood-Horse*](/wiki/The_Blood-Horse "The Blood-Horse") pedigree expert Anne Peters speculated, "Had Sunday Silence retired in Kentucky, it's almost certain he would have tanked commercially and been exported in disgrace, but he found his perfect gene pool and thrived instead."[[4]](#cite_note-4) He would later be the [leading broodmare sire in North America](/wiki/Leading_broodmare_sire_in_North_America "Leading broodmare sire in North America") in 2016.

In the [Blood-Horse magazine List of the Top 100 U.S. Racehorses of the 20th Century](/wiki/Blood-Horse_magazine_List_of_the_Top_100_U.S._Racehorses_of_the_20th_Century "Blood-Horse magazine List of the Top 100 U.S. Racehorses of the 20th Century"), Sunday Silence was ranked #31.

## Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early years")]

Sunday Silence was foaled on March 25, 1986, at [Stone Farm](/wiki/Stone_Farm "Stone Farm") in [Paris, Kentucky](/wiki/Paris,_Kentucky "Paris, Kentucky").[[5]](#cite_note-5) He was sired by Halo out of Wishing Well by Understanding. Though he was registered as a [dark bay](/wiki/Bay_(horse) "Bay (horse)")/[brown](/wiki/Seal_brown_(horse) "Seal brown (horse)"), he was in fact a true black.

He was bred by Oak Cliff Thoroughbreds, Ltd. and escaped death twice: first as a weanling when he nearly died from a freak virus;[[6]](#cite_note-6) and later at age two, traveling in a van when the driver experienced a heart attack and the van flipped over.[[7]](#cite_note-7) He was passed over twice at the sales ring as a yearling before he was sold in California for $50,000 as a two-year-old in training. [Arthur B. Hancock III](/wiki/Arthur_B._Hancock_III "Arthur B. Hancock III") bought him as a "buy-back" (he had bred him), hoping to ship him to Kentucky. However, the van accident kept Sunday Silence in California. [Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Racing_and_Hall_of_Fame "National Museum of Racing and Hall of Fame") trainer [Charlie Whittingham](/wiki/Charlie_Whittingham "Charlie Whittingham") bought a half share of the colt and then sold half of that to Ernest Gaillard. (Ownership designate: [H-G-W Partners](/wiki/H-G-W_Partners "H-G-W Partners").)[[8]](#cite_note-thoroughbredtimes2002-8)

## Ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ownership")]

**H-G-W Partners** (Hancock-Gaillard-Whittingham) represents the names of the three partners who owned the horse.The three partners were:

1. [Arthur B. Hancock III](/wiki/Arthur_B._Hancock_III "Arthur B. Hancock III") (b. 1943) - 50% partner, who is a [horse breeder](/wiki/Horse_breeding "Horse breeding") and the owner of [Stone Farm](/wiki/Stone_Farm "Stone Farm") near [Paris, Kentucky](/wiki/Paris,_Kentucky "Paris, Kentucky").
2. [Charlie Whittingham](/wiki/Charlie_Whittingham "Charlie Whittingham") (1913–1999) - 25% partner, who was the horse's Hall of Fame [trainer](/wiki/Horse_trainer "Horse trainer");
3. [Ernest Gaillard](/w/index.php?title=Ernest_Gaillard&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ernest Gaillard (page does not exist)") (1913–2004) - 25% partner, who was a 1938 graduate of the [University of Louisville](/wiki/University_of_Louisville "University of Louisville") and active in the organizing of the Kentucky Derby, and a medical doctor with the [Eighth Army Air Force](/wiki/Eighth_Air_Force "Eighth Air Force") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

## Racing record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Racing record")]

### 1988: two-year-old season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1988: two-year-old season")]

Although Sunday Silence showed ability, he didn't make it to the races until late in his two-year-old season, finishing second in a maiden race, then winning a maiden special weight race and finishing second in an allowance race from three starts.

### 1989: three-year-old season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1989: three-year-old season")]

Sunday Silence began his three-year-old year by winning an allowance race at Santa Anita by four lengths which opened the door of Kentucky Derby potential. His next race was a victory in the Grade 2 [San Felipe Stakes](/wiki/San_Felipe_Stakes "San Felipe Stakes"), and then he won the G1 [Santa Anita Derby](/wiki/Santa_Anita_Derby "Santa Anita Derby") by eleven lengths to qualify for a start in the [Kentucky Derby](/wiki/Kentucky_Derby "Kentucky Derby").

#### Kentucky Derby

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Kentucky Derby")]

In what became an iconic rivalry, Sunday Silence and Easy Goer would only meet four times, the first of which was the [1989 Kentucky Derby](/wiki/1989_Kentucky_Derby "1989 Kentucky Derby") on May 6. In the buildup to the 1989 [Triple Crown](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing "Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing"), the rivalry developed between the West Coast-based Sunday Silence and the East Coast-based Easy Goer, winner of the 1988 Eclipse Award for Champion Two Year Old Colt. Easy Goer was coming in off of a victory in the Wood Memorial and a record breaking performance in the Gotham Stakes. Unknown to the public however, Easy Goer had a small crack in his left front.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The favorite was Easy Goer, with Sunday Silence as the 3:1 second choice.

[Kentucky Derby](/wiki/Kentucky_Derby "Kentucky Derby") day was a cold one at 44°, the coldest in 72 years, with rain creating a slow muddy track.[[10]](#cite_note-10) After stalking the pace and making his move around the turn, Sunday Silence and jockey [Pat Valenzuela](/wiki/Pat_Valenzuela "Pat Valenzuela") defeated Easy Goer by 2+1⁄2 lengths, in the slowest time (2:05) for a Kentucky Derby since 1958.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Sunday Silence ducked in and out sharply throughout the stretch run, with his jockey switching from left and right handed urging trying to keep him running straight. Even with ducking in and out, the champion Easy Goer was unable to make up ground. *[Daily Racing Form](/wiki/Daily_Racing_Form "Daily Racing Form")* writer Dan Illman stated after Sunday Silence's victory that "the best horse won that afternoon."[[12]](#cite_note-Sunday_Silence,_Derby_talk-12) *Daily Racing Form* chairman Steve Crist stated his opinion that "Easy Goer had a legitimate explanation for his defeat, as he didn't handle the muddy Churchill track."[[13]](#cite_note-13)

#### Preakness Stakes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Preakness Stakes")]

While both horses were preparing for the 1+3⁄16-mile Preakness two weeks after the Derby, each had minor ailments. Sunday Silence came up lame after a gallop seven days before the race. Trainer Whittingham contacted well-known Kentucky veterinarian Alex Harthill, who diagnosed a bruise under the sole, a common injury that "wasn't a serious problem but it had happened at a serious time." Harthill had Sunday Silence step on a clean sheet of white paper which was subsequently faxed to Ric Redden of Lexington, Kentucky, and from which Redden prepared a set of aluminum bar shoes. Redden and his assistant then flew via rented jet to Baltimore with the bar shoes and X-ray machine to confirm that no fracture was involved. After the shoes were fitted, Sunday Silence resumed training four days before the race. After his connections saw the colt's "remarkably" rapid recovery from the injury, the bar shoes were removed the day before the race.[[12]](#cite_note-Sunday_Silence,_Derby_talk-12)[[14]](#cite_note-14) With all the uncertainty over Sunday Silence's soundness, he would go on to be second choice once again to Easy Goer at 2:1.

Meanwhile, unknown to the public at his rival's stable, throughout Preakness week (as late as Friday, the day before the race), Easy Goer's front feet were being soaked in tubs of Epsom salts due to small scratches or cracks on both heels. An ultrasound was also performed on his ankles and knees. Some wondered if these ailments could compromise the chances of both horses.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Easy Goer had "problematic, puffy" ankles that he dealt with throughout his career.

The 1989 Preakness Stakes on May 20 is one that continues to live in racing lore as one of the best races ever run, and one of the most iconic stretch duels. It was added into *Horse Racing's Top 100 Moments*, placing at #70. Easy Goer broke slow, and Sunday Silence was bumped at the start, then the pair settled into their sports. After three-quarters of a mile, Day guided Easy Goer to the front where Sunday Silence dug in and went with him. The legendary stretch duel was fought the entire length of the stretch, with neither Sunday Silence or Easy Goer giving an inch. Sunday Silence prevailed in the photo finish, with a finishing time of 1:53 4/5, the third fastest Preakness at the time.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Some Easy Goer loyalists in the media maintained their horse's superiority, attributing the loss to the fact that Easy Goer had leapt in the air at the start and his jockey, [Pat Day](/wiki/Pat_Day "Pat Day"), reined Easy Goer's head to the right when he had a short lead in the home stretch. Day, who lodged a failed objection against Valenzuela, has called his ride "a mistake."[[18]](#cite_note-Day_finally_steers_Easy_Goer_right-18)

#### Belmont Stakes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Belmont Stakes")]

In 1989, New York was the only state in America that banned all race-day drugs and medications, including the now-commonly used medication Lasix.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) In the three weeks between the Preakness and Belmont, Whittingham was angered that the controversial veterinarian Alex Harthill, who treated Sunday Silence earlier for the Kentucky Derby and Preakness, was not licensed in New York and prohibited from practicing.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) The day before the 1+1⁄2-mile [Belmont Stakes](/wiki/Belmont_Stakes "Belmont Stakes"), Sunday Silence, with exercise rider Pam Mabes up, was spooked and kicked trainer Whittingham in the temple, a glancing blow that came close to killing the trainer.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Belmont Park received several inches of rain in the days leading up to the race,[[25]](#cite_note-25) but by race day on June 10 the track was rated fast with Sunday Silence this time the 4:5 post time favorite, and the entry of Easy Goer and Awe Inspiring at 8:5.[[26]](#cite_note-26) While initially planning on going to the lead, Sunday Silence settled into second behind the longshot French colt Le Voyageur. Easy Goer was never too far behind either. When Sunday Silence made his move on the turn, Easy Goer made a faster one and swept to the front. Easy Goer defeated Sunday Silence by eight lengths in the time of 2:26, producing the second-fastest Belmont Stakes in history, behind only [Secretariat](/wiki/Secretariat_(horse) "Secretariat (horse)"), and denied Sunday Silence the Triple Crown, and thus a $5M bonus.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) However, by virtue of his two Classic wins and his runner-up performance, Sunday Silence was awarded the third $1,000,000 Visa Triple Crown Bonus for best three-year-old in the series.

#### Breeders' Cup Classic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Breeders' Cup Classic")]

After the Belmont Stakes, the pair went their separate ways with Sunday Silence returning to California where he finished second to eventual [Breeders' Cup Turf](/wiki/Breeders%27_Cup_Turf "Breeders' Cup Turf") winner Prized in the Grade II 1+1⁄4-mile [Swaps Stakes](/wiki/Swaps_Stakes "Swaps Stakes") at Hollywood Park on July 23.[[30]](#cite_note-30) From there he went to Louisiana Downs where he won the Grade I [Super Derby](/wiki/Super_Derby "Super Derby") on September 24, giving him six weeks' rest going into the Breeder's Cup Classic. Over in New York, Easy Goer won 4 successive Grade I stakes after the Belmont... the [Whitney Handicap](/wiki/Whitney_Handicap "Whitney Handicap"), [Travers Stakes](/wiki/Travers_Stakes "Travers Stakes"), [Woodward Stakes](/wiki/Woodward_Stakes "Woodward Stakes"), and the [Jockey Club Gold Cup](/wiki/Jockey_Club_Gold_Cup "Jockey Club Gold Cup"), giving him 27 days' rest going into the Classic.

This set up one final face-off between the rivals at the season-ending $3 million 1+1⁄4-mile [Breeders' Cup Classic](/wiki/Breeders%27_Cup_Classic "Breeders' Cup Classic") at [Gulfstream Park](/wiki/Gulfstream_Park "Gulfstream Park"), on November 4. The contest was expected to decide the winner of the [Eclipse Award for Horse of the Year](/wiki/Eclipse_Award_for_Horse_of_the_Year "Eclipse Award for Horse of the Year").[[31]](#cite_note-31) Sunday Silence's jockey [Pat Valenzuela](/wiki/Pat_Valenzuela "Pat Valenzuela") had earlier been suspended for cocaine use and was replaced by Hall of Fame rider [Chris McCarron](/wiki/Chris_McCarron "Chris McCarron"). Sunday Silence was the post time 2:1 second choice behind Easy Goer at 1:2.

The race started as usual for Sunday Silence who settled five lengths behind the leader, but Easy Goer broke slow and was 11 lengths from the front for much of the race. On the backstretch, Sunday Silence inched closer to the lead with Easy Goer noticeably and suddenly getting into stride with three quarters of a mile to go, with track announcer Tom Durkin commenting during the race "he is five lengths behind Sunday Silence and now he's beginning to roll!" and near the half mile pole he continued, "Sunday Silence bracing for the oncoming power of Easy Goer, who's right at his neck!" On the turn however, Sunday Silence continued to gain on the leader, leaving Easy Goer behind. Sunday Silence took control with about an eighth of a mile to go, with Easy Goer three lengths behind. Jockey Chris McCarron continued with a hand ride, and was able to withstand a strong late charge by Easy Goer to win the Classic by a neck. The victory solidified a 3:1 advantage in Sunday Silence's favor.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

At this point, Sunday Silence had earned what was then a single-season record $4.59 million[[33]](#cite_note-Nytimes.com-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) and won seven times in nine starts for the 1989 campaign, earning him [Eclipse Award for Outstanding 3-Year-Old Male Horse](/wiki/Eclipse_Award_for_Outstanding_3-Year-Old_Male_Horse "Eclipse Award for Outstanding 3-Year-Old Male Horse") and [Horse of the Year](/wiki/American_Horse_of_the_Year "American Horse of the Year") honors. For the latter award, Sunday Silence received 223 of 242 votes, making him the most decisive winner since [John Henry](/wiki/John_Henry_(horse) "John Henry (horse)") eight years earlier.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Even with the championship honors, debate continues decades after their careers on who was the better horse.

### 1990: four-year-old season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1990: four-year-old season")]

At the age of four, Sunday Silence won the Californian and placed second in the [Hollywood Gold Cup](/wiki/Hollywood_Gold_Cup "Hollywood Gold Cup") behind [Criminal Type](/wiki/Criminal_Type "Criminal Type") by a head, while giving away 5 pounds.[[39]](#cite_note-39) He suffered an injured ligament that eventually led to his retirement.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Out of 14 career races, he had 9 wins (including 6 Grade 1’s) and 5 runner-up finishes.

### Accomplishments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Accomplishments")]

In 1996, Sunday Silence was inducted into the [National Museum of Racing and Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Racing_and_Hall_of_Fame "National Museum of Racing and Hall of Fame").[[41]](#cite_note-41) He was ranked #31 in the Bloodhorse Top 100 Horses of the 20th Century, while Easy Goer ranked #34. [Blood-Horse](/wiki/Blood-Horse_Publications "Blood-Horse Publications") stated that its rankings "will generate debate for years to come."[[42]](#cite_note-42) The electoral friction was ultimately reflected in the introduction to the Blood-Horse's "Top 100 Racehorses" book, which said, "For all the work and dreaming that went into it... one approaches the list... with a nagging sense of its folly as a rational exercise and of the maddening arbitrariness of its outcome. However, one views this list of horses, whether in peace and contentment—or shock and dismay—all such judgments, of course, are entirely subjective, a mixture of whim, wisdom, and whatever prejudices howl through the back of the mind."[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Since the Breeders’ Cup Classic was instituted in 1984, Alysheba and Sunday Silence were the only two horses to win three legs of a four-race sequence that was defined in 2015 as the [Grand Slam of Thoroughbred racing](/wiki/Grand_Slam_of_Thoroughbred_racing "Grand Slam of Thoroughbred racing"): The Triple Crown races, plus the Breeders' Cup Classic, and Sunday Silence was the first horse to win three legs of the modern Grand Slam in the same year. As the Breeders' Cup began after the 1978 Triple Crown win of Affirmed, the potential for a sweep of all four races only became possible in 1984, and did not occur until 2015 when [American Pharoah](/wiki/American_Pharoah "American Pharoah") won the Triple Crown and eventually the Grand Slam.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## Stud record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Stud record")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Agnes_Tachyon_20081005P2.jpg/250px-Agnes_Tachyon_20081005P2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Agnes_Tachyon_20081005P2.jpg)

[Agnes Tachyon](/wiki/Agnes_Tachyon "Agnes Tachyon")

Sunday Silence was sold to [Japanese](/wiki/Japan "Japan") breeder [Zenya Yoshida](/w/index.php?title=Zenya_Yoshida&action=edit&redlink=1 "Zenya Yoshida (page does not exist)"),[[45]](#cite_note-Hickman-45) to stand at his [Shadai Stallion Station](/wiki/Shadai_Stallion_Station "Shadai Stallion Station") in [Shiraoi, Hokkaido](/wiki/Shiraoi,_Hokkaido "Shiraoi, Hokkaido"). Yoshida had acquired a 25% interest in Sunday Silence early in his 4-year-old season and bought out the other partners for $7.5 million in 1991.[[46]](#cite_note-ACP-46)

Sunday Silence flourished in Japan and became their [leading sire](/wiki/Leading_sire_in_Japan "Leading sire in Japan") from 1995 through 2007,[[45]](#cite_note-Hickman-45) taking over from [Northern Taste](/wiki/Northern_Taste "Northern Taste") (ten-time leading sire in Japan). He was particularly successful with daughters from the [Northern Dancer sire line](/wiki/Northern_Dancer_sire_line "Northern Dancer sire line").[[46]](#cite_note-ACP-46) However, breeders were generally not successful expanding his influence outside of Japan.[[45]](#cite_note-Hickman-45) His progeny have won many races in Japan, including 20 out of 22 JRA Grade 1 flat races (the only exceptions are the [NHK Mile Cup](/wiki/NHK_Mile_Cup "NHK Mile Cup") and the [Japan Cup Dirt](/wiki/Japan_Cup_Dirt "Japan Cup Dirt")). His progeny also have won International Grade 1 race including the [Hong Kong Vase](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Vase "Hong Kong Vase"), [Hong Kong Mile](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Mile "Hong Kong Mile") and [Dubai Sheema Classic](/wiki/Dubai_Sheema_Classic "Dubai Sheema Classic").

Descendants of Sunday Silence have broken many earnings records, in part because he was active at the start of the "big crop" era (siring about 2000 foals) and also because the average purses in Japan are significantly higher than the rest of the world. Conservative estimates on the earnings of Sunday Silence descendants place the total near [JPY](/wiki/Japanese_yen "Japanese yen") 80 billion (approximately $730 million according to Equibase).[[47]](#cite_note-47)

He was also the [leading broodmare sire in North America](/wiki/Leading_broodmare_sire_in_North_America "Leading broodmare sire in North America") in 2016 with Japanese racehorse Lani's entry in the Kentucky Derby that year with a Grade II win in Dubai, followed by off the board finishes in the Derby and Preakness, and a third-place finish in the Belmont.[[48]](#cite_note-Sunday_Silence_Dominates-48) Once qualified to appear on the broodmare sire list, Sunday Silence then got enhancements from his Japanese runners, where there is a substantial disproportion between North American purses and the significantly higher purses in Japan.

### Sire of sires and broodmares

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Sire of sires and broodmares")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Deep_Impact%28horse%29_20051023_3.jpg/250px-Deep_Impact%28horse%29_20051023_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deep_Impact(horse)_20051023_3.jpg)

Deep Impact winning Kikuka Sho 2005 on October 23.

Many of Sunday Silence's sons have gone on to become successful breeding stallions, with at least seventeen of them siring Group or Grade I winners. These include:

* **Fuji Kiseki** sired [Kane Hekili](/wiki/Kane_Hekili "Kane Hekili"), [Straight Girl](/wiki/Straight_Girl "Straight Girl"), [Sun Classique](/w/index.php?title=Sun_Classique&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sun Classique (page does not exist)") (Dubai Sheema Classic) and [Isla Bonita](/wiki/Isla_Bonita "Isla Bonita").
* **Dance in the Dark** sired [Delta Blues](/wiki/Delta_Blues_(horse) "Delta Blues (horse)"), the winner of Australia's [Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2006_Melbourne_Cup "2006 Melbourne Cup").
* **Stay Gold** sired [Orfevre](/wiki/Orfevre "Orfevre"), [Gold Ship](/wiki/Gold_Ship "Gold Ship"), [Dream Journey](/wiki/Dream_Journey "Dream Journey"), [Nakayama Festa](/wiki/Nakayama_Festa "Nakayama Festa"), [Fenomeno](/wiki/Fenomeno_(horse) "Fenomeno (horse)"), [Oju Chosan](/wiki/Oju_Chosan "Oju Chosan"), Red Reveur ([Hanshin Juvenile Fillies](/wiki/Hanshin_Juvenile_Fillies "Hanshin Juvenile Fillies")), Admire Lead (Victoria Mile), [Rainbow Line](/wiki/Rainbow_Line "Rainbow Line"), [Win Bright](/wiki/Win_Bright "Win Bright") and [Indy Champ](/wiki/Indy_Champ "Indy Champ"). Orfevre in turn sired [Marche Lorraine](/wiki/Marche_Lorraine_(horse) "Marche Lorraine (horse)") and [Ushba Tesoro](/wiki/Ushba_Tesoro "Ushba Tesoro"), while Gold Ship sired [Uberleben](/wiki/Uberleben "Uberleben"), Meiner Grand ([Nakayama Daishogai](/wiki/Nakayama_Daishogai "Nakayama Daishogai")), and [Meisho Tabaru](/wiki/Meisho_Tabaru "Meisho Tabaru").
* **Agnes Gold** sired Silence Is Gold, Abu Dhabi, Mais Que Bonita, Abidjan, Antonella Baby, Ivar, Energia Fribby, Hevea, Honra Real, Nathan, Olympic Kremlin, Culo e Camicia, Olympic Jhonsnow, Orfeu Negro, Olympic Las Palmas, Janelle Monae, In Love, Online, Look Of Love, No Fear, Doutor Sureño and Neverwalkalone.
* **Divine Light** sired [Natagora](/wiki/Natagora "Natagora").
* **Special Week** sired Toho Jackal (Kikuka Sho), [Buena Vista](/wiki/Buena_Vista_(horse) "Buena Vista (horse)") and [Cesario](/wiki/Cesario_(horse) "Cesario (horse)"); who in turn foaled [Epiphaneia](/wiki/Epiphaneia_(horse) "Epiphaneia (horse)"), [Leontes](/wiki/Leontes_(horse) "Leontes (horse)"), and [Saturnalia](/wiki/Saturnalia_(horse) "Saturnalia (horse)").
* **Manhattan Cafe** sired Grape Brandy ([February Stakes](/wiki/February_Stakes "February Stakes")), Hiruno d'Amour (Tenno Sho), Jo Cappuccino (NHK Mile Cup), [Red Desire](/wiki/Red_Desire "Red Desire") and Queens Ring ([Queen Elizabeth II Commemorative Cup](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II_Commemorative_Cup "Queen Elizabeth II Commemorative Cup")).
* **Agnes Tachyon** sired Logic (NHK Mile Cup), [Daiwa Scarlet](/wiki/Daiwa_Scarlet "Daiwa Scarlet"), [Deep Sky](/wiki/Deep_Sky_(horse) "Deep Sky (horse)"), Captain Thule (Satsuki Sho), Little Amapola (Queen Elizabeth II Cup) and [Reve d'Essor](/wiki/Reve_d%27Essor "Reve d'Essor").
* **Neo Universe** sired [Unrivaled](/w/index.php?title=Unrivaled_(horse)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Unrivaled (horse) (page does not exist)") (Satsuki Shō), [Logi Universe](/wiki/Logi_Universe "Logi Universe"), [Victoire Pisa](/wiki/Victoire_Pisa "Victoire Pisa"), and Neorealism. Victoire Pisa in turn sired the Oka Sho winner [Jeweler](/wiki/Jeweler_(horse) "Jeweler (horse)").
* **Zenno Rob Roy** sired Saint Emilion (Yūshun Himba)
* **Daiwa Major** sired Curren Black Hill ([NHK Mile Cup](/wiki/NHK_Mile_Cup "NHK Mile Cup")), [Major Emblem](/wiki/Major_Emblem "Major Emblem"), [Admire Mars](/wiki/Admire_Mars "Admire Mars"), [Resistencia](/wiki/Resistencia_(horse) "Resistencia (horse)"), Serifos, Double Major, and [Ascoli Piceno](/wiki/Ascoli_Piceno_(horse) "Ascoli Piceno (horse)").
* **Hat Trick** sired [Dabirsim](/wiki/Dabirsim "Dabirsim") and King David ([Jamaica Handicap](/wiki/Jamaica_Handicap "Jamaica Handicap")).
* **Deep Impact** sired [Gentildonna](/wiki/Gentildonna "Gentildonna"), [Deep Brillante](/wiki/Deep_Brillante "Deep Brillante"), [Kizuna](/wiki/Kizuna_(horse) "Kizuna (horse)"), [Harp Star](/wiki/Harp_Star "Harp Star"), [A Shin Hikari](/wiki/A_Shin_Hikari "A Shin Hikari"), [Makahiki](/wiki/Makahiki_(horse) "Makahiki (horse)"), [Shonan Pandora](/wiki/Shonan_Pandora "Shonan Pandora"), Beauty Parlour ([Poule d'Essai des Pouliches](/wiki/Poule_d%27Essai_des_Pouliches "Poule d'Essai des Pouliches")), [Ayusan](/wiki/Ayusan "Ayusan"), [Verxina](/wiki/Verxina "Verxina"), [Tosen Ra](/wiki/Tosen_Ra_(horse) "Tosen Ra (horse)"), [Mikki Isle](/wiki/Mikki_Isle "Mikki Isle"), [Dee Majesty](/wiki/Dee_Majesty "Dee Majesty"), Spielberg (Tennō Shō), Lachesis (Queen Elizabeth II Commemorative Cup), Danon Shark (Mile Championship), [Shonan Adela](/wiki/Shonan_Adela "Shonan Adela"), [Danon Platina](/wiki/Danon_Platina "Danon Platina"), Real Impact, [Mikki Queen](/wiki/Mikki_Queen "Mikki Queen"), [Marialite](/wiki/Marialite_(horse) "Marialite (horse)"), [Real Steel](/wiki/Real_Steel_(horse) "Real Steel (horse)"), [Sinhalite](/wiki/Sinhalite_(horse) "Sinhalite (horse)"), [Danon Premium](/wiki/Danon_Premium "Danon Premium"), [Vivlos](/wiki/Vivlos "Vivlos"), [Satono Diamond](/wiki/Satono_Diamond "Satono Diamond"), [Al Ain](/wiki/Al_Ain_(horse) "Al Ain (horse)"), [Saxon Warrior](/wiki/Saxon_Warrior "Saxon Warrior"), [Study of Man](/wiki/Study_of_Man "Study of Man"), [Fierement](/wiki/Fierement "Fierement"), Fierce Impact, [Wagnerian](/wiki/Wagnerian_(horse) "Wagnerian (horse)"), [Gran Alegria](/wiki/Gran_Alegria "Gran Alegria"), [Roger Barows](/wiki/Roger_Barows "Roger Barows"), [Loves Only You](/wiki/Loves_Only_You "Loves Only You"), [Contrail](/wiki/Contrail_(horse) "Contrail (horse)"), [Fancy Blue](/wiki/Fancy_Blue "Fancy Blue"), [Ask Victor More](/wiki/Ask_Victor_More "Ask Victor More"), and [Auguste Rodin](/wiki/Auguste_Rodin_(horse) "Auguste Rodin (horse)"). Real Steel in turn sired Saudi Cup and Breeders' Cup Classic winner [Forever Young](/wiki/Forever_Young_(horse) "Forever Young (horse)").
* **Suzuka Phoenix** sired Meiner Ho O ([NHK Mile Cup](/wiki/NHK_Mile_Cup "NHK Mile Cup"))
* **Gold Allure** sired [Espoir City](/wiki/Espoir_City "Espoir City"), [Smart Falcon](/wiki/Smart_Falcon "Smart Falcon"), [Copano Rickey](/wiki/Copano_Rickey "Copano Rickey"), Gold Dream (February Stakes), Chrysoberyl (Japan Cup Dirt, [Champions Cup](/wiki/Champions_Cup_(horse_race) "Champions Cup (horse race)")), and [Naran Huleg](/wiki/Naran_Huleg "Naran Huleg").
* **Heart's Cry** sired [Just A Way](/wiki/Just_A_Way "Just A Way"), [Admire Rakti](/wiki/Admire_Rakti "Admire Rakti"), [Cheval Grand](/wiki/Cheval_Grand "Cheval Grand"), Nuovo Record (Yūshun Himba), [One And Only](/wiki/One_and_Only_(horse) "One and Only (horse)"), [Lys Gracieux](/wiki/Lys_Gracieux "Lys Gracieux"), [Suave Richard](/wiki/Suave_Richard "Suave Richard"), Yoshida, Time Flyer, [Salios](/wiki/Salios "Salios"), [Do Deuce](/wiki/Do_Deuce "Do Deuce"), and [Continuous](/wiki/Continuous_(horse) "Continuous (horse)").
* **Black Tide** sired [Kitasan Black](/wiki/Kitasan_Black "Kitasan Black") and [Kamunyak](/wiki/Kamunyak_(horse) "Kamunyak (horse)"). Kitasan Black in turn sired [Equinox](/wiki/Equinox_(horse) "Equinox (horse)"), [Sol Oriens](/wiki/Sol_Oriens "Sol Oriens"), and [Croix du Nord](/wiki/Croix_du_Nord "Croix du Nord").
* **Suzuka Mambo** sired [Meisho Mambo](/wiki/Meisho_Mambo "Meisho Mambo") and Sambista ([Champions Cup](/wiki/Champions_Cup_(horse_race) "Champions Cup (horse race)"))

In addition to his sons, his daughter Sun is Up was the dam of [2014 Breeders' Cup](/wiki/2014_Breeders%27_Cup "2014 Breeders' Cup") [Mile](/wiki/Breeders%27_Cup_Mile "Breeders' Cup Mile") winner [Karakontie](/wiki/Karakontie "Karakontie").[[49]](#cite_note-49) Sunday Silence is also the damsire of [Screen Hero](/wiki/Screen_Hero "Screen Hero") (foaled by Running Heroine), [Duramente](/wiki/Duramente "Duramente") (foaled by Admire Groove), and [Almond Eye](/wiki/Almond_Eye "Almond Eye") (foaled by Fusaichi Pandora), among others.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

When *[Blood-Horse](/wiki/Blood-Horse "Blood-Horse")* magazine started to include Japanese earnings in their stallion rankings in 2016, Sunday Silence was the leading broodmare sire of the year.[[53]](#cite_note-53) In 2022, Gendarme (a grandson of Sunday Silence through his daughter Believe) won the G1 [Sprinters Stakes](/wiki/Sprinters_Stakes "Sprinters Stakes"), the same race his dam won in 2002.[[54]](#cite_note-54) In 2025, [Forever Young](/wiki/Forever_Young_(horse) "Forever Young (horse)") (a great grandson of Sunday Silence through his son [Deep Impact](/wiki/Deep_Impact_(horse) "Deep Impact (horse)")) won the 2025 [Breeders' Cup Classic](/wiki/Breeders%27_Cup_Classic "Breeders' Cup Classic"), the first win in the Classic for Japan.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sunday_Silence&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Death")]

Sunday Silence died on August 19, 2002. He had been treated for [laminitis](/wiki/Laminitis "Laminitis") for the previous 14 weeks and had developed an infection in one leg as well. He had been given a stronger dose of a different painkilling medication the previous day to provide him relief, and apparently as a result, he had become comfortable enough to lie down for the first time in a week. The following morning, he appeared unable to rise, and while veterinarians were discussing what to do, he died, apparently of heart failure.[[56]](#cite_note-56)
